disclaimer: Pediatric dosing reference for Community Health Workers. Always verify with local clinical guidelines and refer to a clinician if uncertain. Doses are pediatric unless otherwise noted. Weight bands round to the standard tablet/syrup increment shown in 'notes'. Never exceed the stated maximum daily dose.

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.ageRestrictions: Safe from neonate at 10 mg/kg/dose. Use lower dose in jaundice or liver disease.
medications.contraindications: Severe hepatic impairment. Do not exceed 60 mg/kg/day.
medications.notes: Syrup 120 mg/5 mL; tablet 500 mg. Round to nearest 2.5 mL of syrup. First-line antipyretic in children.
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Annex 2: Drug dosages
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 2.1

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.maxDailyDose: 30 mg/kg/day in children; adult max 1200 mg/day OTC, 2400 mg/day prescription
medications.ageRestrictions: Not recommended under 3 months or under 5 kg. Avoid in dehydration.
medications.contraindications: Active GI bleed, severe dehydration, renal impairment, known NSAID allergy, severe asthma triggered by NSAIDs, dengue (bleeding risk).
medications.notes: Suspension 100 mg/5 mL; tablet 200 mg or 400 mg. Give with food. Avoid in suspected dengue or severe diarrhoea.
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Annex 2
BNF for Children (current edition), Ibuprofen monograph

medications.name: Amoxicillin
medications.frequency: twice daily for 5 days (40 mg/kg/dose, WHO pneumonia regimen)
medications.maxDailyDose: 80-90 mg/kg/day; adult max 3 g/day in most indications
medications.ageRestrictions: Safe from neonate with appropriate dose adjustment. Reduce dose in severe renal impairment.
medications.contraindications: Known penicillin allergy. Caution with infectious mononucleosis (high rash incidence).
medications.notes: Dispersible tablet 250 mg; suspension 125 mg/5 mL or 250 mg/5 mL. WHO first-line for non-severe fast-breathing pneumonia: 40 mg/kg/dose twice daily x 5 days. Round to nearest tablet. The 40 mg/kg per-dose figure is for TWICE-daily dosing only (80 mg/kg/day). Do not give this dose three times daily.
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Ch. 4 Cough/difficulty in breathing; Annex 2
WHO Revised Classification and Treatment of Pneumonia in Children at Health Facilities (2014)
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.2.1

medications.name: Co-trimoxazole (Sulfamethoxazole-Trimethoprim)
indications: alternative for pneumonia where amoxicillin unavailable
medications.maxDailyDose: Adult: 320 mg TMP / 1600 mg SMX per day in 2 divided doses
medications.ageRestrictions: Avoid in infants under 6 weeks (risk of kernicterus). Use with caution in G6PD deficiency.
medications.contraindications: Sulfa allergy, severe hepatic or renal impairment, infants under 6 weeks, last trimester of pregnancy.
medications.notes: Dose expressed as trimethoprim (TMP) component; standard 5:1 ratio with sulfamethoxazole (SMX). Paediatric suspension 40 mg TMP + 200 mg SMX per 5 mL. Adult single-strength tablet 80 mg TMP + 400 mg SMX.
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.2.1

medications.name: Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS, low-osmolarity)
medications.frequency: Plan B: 75 mL/kg over 4 hours, then reassess. Plan A: after each loose stool give 50-100 mL (under 2 y) or 100-200 mL (2-10 y).
medications.maxDailyDose: Adjust to ongoing losses; no fixed upper limit, but watch for fluid overload in cardiac or renal disease.
medications.ageRestrictions: Safe at all ages. For infants under 6 months, continue breastfeeding alongside ORS.
sideEffects: vomiting if given too fast (slow the rate)
medications.contraindications: Severe dehydration with shock (use IV fluids first), persistent vomiting unresponsive to slow administration, intestinal obstruction, ileus.
medications.notes: Low-osmolarity formula (245 mOsm/L). Values shown are mL of reconstituted solution for Plan B (75 mL/kg over 4 h). Continue breastfeeding. Give in small sips with a spoon or cup. Add zinc supplementation.
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Ch. 5 Diarrhoea, Chart 14: Plan B
IMCI Chart Booklet (WHO/UNICEF, 2014 update), Treat the Child section
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 26.2

medications.name: Zinc sulfate
medications.maxDailyDose: 20 mg/day in children 6 months and older; 10 mg/day under 6 months
medications.ageRestrictions: Under 6 months: 10 mg/day (approx weight under 7 kg). 6 months and older: 20 mg/day.
medications.notes: Dispersible tablet 20 mg. For infants under 6 months give half a 20 mg tablet (10 mg) dispersed in breast milk or ORS. Continue full course even after diarrhoea stops. AGE-BASED DOSING: 10 mg once daily under 6 months; 20 mg once daily 6 months and older, for 10-14 days (WHO/UNICEF).
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Ch. 5 Diarrhoea
WHO/UNICEF Joint Statement: Clinical management of acute diarrhoea (2004)
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 17.5.2

medications.name: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL)
medications.unit: mg artemether (= number of 20/120 mg tablets x 20)
medications.frequency: 6-dose regimen at 0, 8, 24, 36, 48 and 60 hours over 3 days
medications.maxDailyDose: 4 tablets per dose (adult dose); do not repeat course within 1 month except on clinician advice
medications.route: oral, with fat (milk, breast milk, or food) to ensure absorption
medications.ageRestrictions: Use from 5 kg body weight. Not recommended in first trimester of pregnancy unless no alternative.
medications.contraindications: Severe malaria (use parenteral artesunate). Known cardiac arrhythmia or QT prolongation. First trimester pregnancy (relative).
medications.notes: Tablet 20 mg artemether + 120 mg lumefantrine. Weight bands: 5 to under 15 kg = 1 tab/dose; 15 to under 25 kg = 2 tabs/dose; 25 to under 35 kg = 3 tabs/dose; 35 kg or more = 4 tabs/dose. Repeat the dose if vomited within 30 min. Give with fatty food to improve absorption.
sources: WHO Guidelines for the Treatment of Malaria, 3rd ed. (2015), section 6 Treatment of uncomplicated malaria
WHO Guidelines for malaria (consolidated, latest revision), Annex on antimalarial dosing
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.5.3

medications.name: Mebendazole
medications.frequency: Single 500 mg dose for preventive chemotherapy; or 100 mg twice daily for 3 days for confirmed mixed infection
medications.ageRestrictions: Use from 12 months of age. Avoid in first trimester of pregnancy.
medications.contraindications: Under 12 months. First trimester of pregnancy. Known hypersensitivity.
medications.notes: Chewable tablet 500 mg or 100 mg. Single 500 mg dose is the WHO preventive chemotherapy standard for children 12 months and older. Children under 1 year: do not give (use community deworming programme guidance).
sources: WHO Guideline: Preventive chemotherapy to control soil-transmitted helminth infections in at-risk population groups (2017)
WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.1.1

medications.name: Albendazole
medications.frequency: Single dose; repeat at 6-12 month intervals in endemic areas
medications.ageRestrictions: 12-23 months: 200 mg (half tablet). 24 months and older: 400 mg. Avoid in first trimester of pregnancy.
medications.contraindications: Under 12 months. First trimester of pregnancy. Known hypersensitivity.
medications.notes: Chewable tablet 400 mg (scored). Children 12-23 months receive half tablet (200 mg).
sources: WHO Guideline: Preventive chemotherapy to control soil-transmitted helminth infections in at-risk population groups (2017)
WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.1.1

medications.name: Vitamin A
medications.frequency: Every 4-6 months for prevention. For measles or xerophthalmia: give same dose on day 1, day 2 and at 2-4 weeks.
medications.maxDailyDose: Per age band; do not repeat preventive dose within 1 month.
medications.ageRestrictions: Under 6 months: 50,000 IU. 6-11 months: 100,000 IU. 12 months and older: 200,000 IU.
medications.contraindications: Hypervitaminosis A. Pregnancy (high-dose). Recent high-dose vitamin A within the past month.
medications.notes: Capsules 50,000 / 100,000 / 200,000 IU. For measles, give a second dose the next day and a third at 2-4 weeks if eye signs of vitamin A deficiency are present. AGE-BASED DOSING: 50,000 IU under 6 months; 100,000 IU 6-11 months; 200,000 IU 12 months and older (WHO). Never dose by weight.
sources: WHO Guideline: Vitamin A supplementation in infants and children 6-59 months of age (2011)
WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Ch. 6 Measles, Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 27.5

medications.name: Ferrous Sulfate
medications.frequency: once daily (or in 2-3 divided doses), between meals if tolerated
medications.maxDailyDose: 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron, max 60 mg/day in young children; adult 60-120 mg/day
medications.ageRestrictions: From 6 months of age for treatment of anaemia. Do not give to children with severe acute malnutrition until stabilisation phase complete.
medications.contraindications: Haemochromatosis, haemoglobinopathies with iron overload, severe acute malnutrition in stabilisation phase, active infection in some malaria-endemic settings (per local guidance).
medications.notes: Drops 25 mg elemental iron per mL; tablet 60 mg elemental iron (200 mg ferrous sulfate). Dose expressed as elemental iron. Continue for 3 months after haemoglobin normalises to replenish stores. Rinse mouth after syrup to reduce staining.
sources: WHO Guideline: Daily iron supplementation in infants and children (2016)
WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 10.1

medications.name: Metronidazole
medications.frequency: three times daily for 5-7 days (giardiasis) or 7-10 days (amoebiasis/anaerobes)
medications.ageRestrictions: Use from neonate with adjusted dose. Avoid alcohol during and 48 h after course.
medications.contraindications: First trimester of pregnancy (relative). Known hypersensitivity. Concurrent alcohol use.
medications.notes: Suspension 200 mg/5 mL (as benzoate); tablet 200 mg or 400 mg. For amoebic dysentery, dose is higher (10 mg/kg three times daily) per WHO Pocket Book. Avoid alcohol.
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Ch. 5 Diarrhoea (amoebiasis/giardiasis), Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.1.2 and 6.2.2

medications.name: Ciprofloxacin
medications.frequency: twice daily for 3 days (dysentery); 7-10 days for other indications
medications.maxDailyDose: Children: 30 mg/kg/day, max 1 g/day. Adult: 1.5 g/day oral.
medications.ageRestrictions: Reserved antibiotic. Use when bloody diarrhoea persists or when Shigella is suspected/confirmed. Theoretical risk of arthropathy in growing children: benefits outweigh risk in serious infection.
medications.contraindications: Known fluoroquinolone allergy. Caution in epilepsy. Avoid concurrent dairy or antacids within 2 h.
medications.notes: Tablet 250 mg or 500 mg; suspension 250 mg/5 mL. WHO first-line for shigellosis: 15 mg/kg twice daily for 3 days. Do not give with milk, antacids or iron at the same time.
sources: WHO Guidelines for the control of shigellosis, including epidemics due to Shigella dysenteriae type 1 (2005)
WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Ch. 5 Bloody diarrhoea, Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.2.2

medications.name: Erythromycin
indications: alternative for penicillin allergy in respiratory/skin infection
medications.frequency: four times daily (every 6 hours), typically for 7-14 days (14 days for pertussis)
medications.ageRestrictions: Caution in neonates (risk of infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis with prolonged use, especially under 2 weeks).
medications.contraindications: Known macrolide allergy. Concurrent use with QT-prolonging drugs. Severe hepatic impairment.
medications.notes: Tablet 250 mg; suspension 125 mg/5 mL or 250 mg/5 mL. Pertussis treatment is 14 days. Take with food if GI upset.
sources: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, 2nd ed. (2013), Ch. 4 Pertussis, Annex 2
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.2.2

medications.name: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate
medications.frequency: twice daily for 5-10 days depending on indication
medications.maxDailyDose: 50 mg/kg/day amoxicillin component standard; up to 90 mg/kg/day for severe infections. Adult max 1750 mg/day amoxicillin component (875 mg twice daily).
medications.ageRestrictions: Safe from neonate with dose adjustment. Reduce dose in significant renal impairment.
medications.contraindications: Known penicillin allergy. Previous amoxicillin-clavulanate-associated jaundice or hepatic dysfunction.
medications.notes: Doses expressed as amoxicillin component. Common ratios 4:1 or 7:1 (paediatric suspensions) and 14:1 (high-dose). Standard suspension 400 mg amoxicillin + 57 mg clavulanate per 5 mL. Always check the ratio on the local product.
sources: BNF for Children (current edition), Co-amoxiclav monograph
WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for Children, 9th list (2023), section 6.2.1
